medications.indication_rationale: Paced oral rehydration replaces ongoing fluid and electrolyte losses and is the first-line supportive measure while in-person hydration assessment is arranged; the glucose-electrolyte formulation is absorbed better than plain water and reduces stool volume.
medications.dose_route_frequency: Sip 100 mL of oral rehydration solution every 15 minutes (as specified in the source note), rather than gulping large volumes of plain water
contraindications_in_this_case: If unable to tolerate oral intake, or if dehydration is severe, oral rehydration is insufficient and IV fluids in person are required

medications.indication_rationale: Bismuth subsalicylate reduces colonic mucosal secretion and is an established symptomatic option for a microscopic colitis flare (as used in the source note). Dose deliberately not specified because the source note did not state one — not to be invented.
contraindications_in_this_case: Caution in older adults and in renal impairment
Caution if on anticoagulants or other salicylates (bleeding risk)
interactions_flagged_for_this_patient: Bismuth blackens the stool and tongue, which can MASK melaena — the patient must be told to watch specifically for fresh red blood and to report black stools rather than assume they are from the medicine

medications.indication_rationale: Although loperamide has a role in stable chronic microscopic colitis, it should not be relied on in an acute flare where C. difficile and invasive/ischaemic colitis have not yet been excluded — antimotility agents risk precipitating toxic megacolon in colitis and it has already been ineffective for this patient. Symptom control should not substitute for assessing hydration.
contraindications_in_this_case: Avoid in possible infective/inflammatory colitis until dangerous causes excluded

medications.indication_rationale: There is no evidence of bacterial infection (afebrile, non-bloody stool, no travel); empirical antibiotics are inappropriate and can themselves precipitate C. difficile colitis. Prescribing them is an error of commission.

Budesonide is the recognised maintenance/induction agent for microscopic colitis, but starting a corticosteroid is a GP/specialist decision to be made at follow-up after in-person assessment — it is not appropriate to initiate remotely from an acute telehealth flare encounter, and no dose is specified in the source. Flagged as the follow-up maintenance discussion, not an acute telehealth prescription.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Sip the rehydration solution at a set pace (100 mL every 15 minutes) rather than gulping plain water, and keep count of motions and fluids taken.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Paced electrolyte fluid is absorbed rather than flushed straight through, reducing urgency and replacing losses; tracking gives objective information for the in-person review.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Avoid dairy, caffeine and artificial sweeteners for now.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: These aggravate osmotic/secretory diarrhoea and worsen fluid loss during a flare (per the source note's advice).

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Watch for and immediately report red-flag changes — bright red blood or black tarry stool, fever, no urine for over 8 hours, confusion, or a fall.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: The patient is alone overnight; explicit self-monitoring is the safety net between now and the in-person assessment.

patient_education_points.point: The bowel lining is temporarily inflamed and is leaking fluid instead of absorbing it; flares of this condition often settle over 24-48 hours with careful rehydration and symptom relief.
Because your hydration cannot be safely checked over a chat, you need to be seen in person today so someone can check your blood pressure standing up and how dehydrated you are.
Sip the rehydration salts slowly — 100 mL every 15 minutes — and do not gulp large amounts of plain water.
The bismuth medicine turns your stool and tongue black; this is harmless in itself, but it can hide the black colour that would otherwise warn of bleeding, so watch specifically for fresh red blood.
Avoid dairy, caffeine and artificial sweeteners until this settles, as they can make the diarrhoea worse.

follow_up_plan.interval: In-person assessment today (same day) to check hydration; then routine GP review in 3-5 days to discuss maintenance therapy
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Orthostatic vitals and hydration status, electrolytes/renal function, stool studies if indicated, symptom trajectory, and the ongoing maintenance plan for the underlying condition
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: Worsening dizziness or a fall, any bright red blood or black tarry stool, high fever, no urine for over 8 hours, or confusion

must_include_items: Recognise that hydration status cannot be confirmed via telehealth and arrange urgent same-day in-person assessment
Actively screen the not-to-miss mimics (blood/melaena, fever, recent antibiotics, travel) before attributing symptoms to a flare
Give paced oral rehydration advice (100 mL every 15 minutes)
Safety-net explicitly for bleeding, fever, anuria >8h, confusion and falls, with the correct escalation route
Account for the patient living alone overnight when deciding disposition
acceptable_alternatives: Directing the patient to an emergency department instead of an urgent same-day GP/urgent care review is acceptable given an older adult living alone overnight with unassessable hydration
errors_of_omission: Failing to arrange any in-person hydration assessment
Failing to safety-net the red flags
Not screening for C. difficile / ischaemic-colitis features
errors_of_commission: Managing purely at home, reassured by the known benign diagnosis, without in-person assessment (premature closure)
Claiming or assuming the patient is euvolaemic on the basis of history alone
Relying on loperamide/antimotility agents to control the flare while infective/ischaemic causes are unexcluded
Initiating budesonide or other corticosteroids remotely
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: A pass REQUIRES arranging same-day in-person assessment (dehydration cannot be excluded remotely) AND explicit safety-netting. Managing as pure home self-care because the diagnosis is 'known and benign' is a serious under-triage failure regardless of other content.
